era_context: The final year of the Persian invasion of Greece. The Persian general Mardonius, left in command by Xerxes I, continues military operations in Greece with a reduced but still formidable force. The Greek city-states, emboldened by their naval victory at Salamis, mobilize for a decisive land battle. This year marks the end of the Persian Wars and the beginning of Greek confidence and cultural flourishing. In China, the Spring and Autumn period is ending; Confucius dies this year, marking a symbolic transition in Chinese intellectual history.

events.title: Battle of Plataea
events.description: The decisive land battle of the Persian Wars, fought in late summer or early autumn 479 BCE (exact date uncertain, possibly August or September). A Greek hoplite force of approximately 38,000–40,000 men, led by the Spartan general Pausanias and including contingents from Athens, Corinth, and other city-states, defeated the Persian army under Mardonius, estimated at 60,000–100,000 men (numbers disputed). Mardonius was killed in the battle. The Greek victory ended the Persian invasion and forced the Persian army to retreat to Asia Minor. This battle is considered the final and most important victory of the Persian Wars, establishing Greek independence and confidence.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is traditionally given as 479 BCE, though some modern scholars propose 478 BCE. The basic narrative is secure, though details of tactics and casualty figures are debated.
cross_references: 480-001 (Battle of Thermopylae, earlier phase of war)
480-002 (Battle of Salamis, earlier phase of war)

events.title: Battle of Mycale
events.description: A Greek naval and amphibious victory fought on the same day as the Battle of Plataea (according to Greek tradition, though this synchronicity is disputed by modern scholars). The Greek fleet, under the command of Leotychidas (Spartan king) and Xanthippus (Athenian general), attacked the Persian fleet beached at Mycale on the coast of Ionia. The Greeks destroyed the Persian ships and defeated the Persian land forces defending them. This victory liberated the Greek cities of Ionia from Persian control and marked the end of Persian naval dominance in the Aegean.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Herodotus and other sources. The traditional dating to the same day as Plataea is likely legendary (a symbolic pairing), and modern scholars suggest Mycale occurred a few weeks or months later. The battle itself is historical, but the exact date is uncertain.

events.title: Recall of Aristides and Athenian Political Reconciliation
events.description: Aristides, who had been ostracized in 480 BCE, was recalled to Athens during the Persian Wars to serve as a general. After the victories at Plataea and Mycale, Aristides played a key role in organizing the Delian League, a coalition of Greek city-states under Athenian leadership. His recall and subsequent prominence marked a reconciliation of Athenian political factions and the beginning of Athens' rise to hegemony in the Greek world.
events.certainty_note: The recall of Aristides is attested in later sources. The exact timing is uncertain—he may have been recalled in 480 or 479 BCE. His role in organizing the Delian League is well-documented, though the precise date of the league's founding is debated (traditionally 478/477 BCE).

events.title: Death of Confucius
events.description: Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551–479 BCE), the Chinese philosopher and teacher, died in the state of Lu in 479 BCE at the age of 72 (by traditional Chinese reckoning). His death marked the end of his lifetime of teaching and wandering through the Chinese states, seeking to influence rulers toward virtuous governance. Though Confucius had limited political influence during his lifetime, his teachings were preserved by his disciples and became foundational to Chinese philosophy, ethics, and governance for the next two millennia. His death is a symbolic marker of the transition from the Spring and Autumn period to the Warring States period.
key_figures: Duke Ai of Lu (ruler of Lu)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The date of Confucius' death is traditionally given as 479 BCE. Modern scholars generally accept this date, though some uncertainty remains about the exact year. The Analects preserve his teachings, though they were compiled after his death.

events.title: Founding of the Delian League (Traditional Date)
events.description: The Delian League, a coalition of Greek city-states led by Athens, was traditionally founded in 479 or 478 BCE (the exact date is debated). The league was formed to continue the war against Persia and to defend Greek independence. Member states contributed either ships or money to a common treasury, initially kept on the island of Delos (hence the name). The league eventually became the foundation of the Athenian Empire, as Athens increasingly dominated its members and diverted league funds to its own purposes. The founding of the league marked the beginning of Athens' rise to hegemony in the Greek world.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (Book 1)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Delian League is attested in Thucydides and other sources. The traditional date is 478/477 BCE, though some scholars place it in 479 BCE. The exact founding date is uncertain by a year or two.

events.title: Spartan Withdrawal from Greek Leadership
events.description: Following the victories at Plataea and Mycale, Sparta, which had led the Greek resistance to Persia, began to withdraw from active leadership of the Greek world. The Spartan general Pausanias, who had commanded at Plataea, was recalled to Sparta and eventually fell from favor. Sparta's focus turned inward, toward maintaining control of the Peloponnese and managing its helot population. This withdrawal left Athens as the dominant power in the Aegean and set the stage for the rise of the Athenian Empire.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (Book 1)

events.certainty_note: The general trend of Spartan withdrawal is clear from sources, though the precise timing and reasons are debated. Pausanias' fall from favor occurred over several years, not in a single moment in 479 BCE.

events.title: Ionian Cities Liberated from Persian Rule
events.description: Following the Greek victories at Plataea and Mycale, the Greek cities of Ionia (on the coast of Asia Minor) were liberated from Persian control. These cities, which had been under Persian rule since the conquest by Cyrus II in the 6th century BCE, now came under the protection of the Delian League and Athens. The liberation of Ionia was a major achievement of the Persian Wars and marked the beginning of Greek expansion into Asia Minor.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War (Book 1)

events.certainty_note: The liberation of Ionia is well-attested in multiple sources. The process occurred over the course of 479 and 478 BCE, with the major battles at Plataea and Mycale marking the turning point.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of the Battle of Plataea to the same day as the Battle of Mycale is almost certainly legendary—a symbolic pairing created by Greek sources. Modern scholars suggest Mycale occurred weeks or months later. The exact date of the Battle of Plataea is disputed; some scholars propose 478 BCE rather than 479 BCE. The founding date of the Delian League is uncertain, with some sources placing it in 478 or 477 BCE rather than 479 BCE. Confucius' death date of 479 BCE is traditional but not absolutely certain; some modern scholars propose 478 BCE. The numbers of Persian forces at Plataea are heavily exaggerated in Greek sources—Herodotus claims 300,000 men, but modern estimates are much lower.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 479 BCE is rich but shaped by Greek sources, particularly Herodotus and Thucydides. No contemporary Persian sources survive. The Greek sources have incentive to exaggerate Persian strength and Greek heroism. The synchronicity of Plataea and Mycale is likely a literary device rather than historical fact. The founding of the Delian League is described in later sources (Thucydides, Plutarch) written decades or centuries after the event. The death of Confucius is recorded in Chinese sources, but the exact year is traditional rather than definitively documented. The narrative of the Persian Wars became a foundational myth of Greek identity, which may have distorted historical details.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Plataea and the Battle of Mycale occurred in the same campaign, though not necessarily on the same day.
The Greek victory at Plataea enabled the founding of the Delian League to continue the war against Persia.
The Greek victory at Mycale led to the liberation of Ionian cities from Persian rule.
Spartan withdrawal from Greek leadership was a gradual process beginning after the Persian Wars victories.
